title: Low-Man Rotation: The Last Line of Defense

lesson.title: When the play breaks, the low man saves it
lesson.body_md:
## Who is the low man?

The low man is the **weak-side defender closest to the rim**. When the offense beats the first line of defense, the low man is the one who has to rotate over and stop the layup. If they don't rotate, it's two points every time.

## The rotation rule

> *"When the ball gets to the rim, the low man tags the roller or stops the ball."*

This comes up most on:
- A driving wing who beats their defender
- A pick-and-roll where the screener is rolling hard to the rim
- A baseline drive that gets past the help defender

## The 3-step rotation

1. **Recognize.** As soon as the ball turns the corner or the roller starts diving, you're the low man — your job has changed.
2. **Tag or take.** Either tag the roller (poke or hit them so they can't catch clean) **or** fully commit to stopping the ball.
3. **Trust the next rotation.** Your man is now open. Your weak-side teammate has to rotate to them. Defense is dominoes.

## When to take the ball vs. tag the roller

- **Roller is wide open under the rim?** *Tag the roller.* The driver still has a tough finish.
- **Driver has a clear lane and the roller is covered?** *Take the ball.* Force a kick-out.
- **Both threats live?** *Take the bigger one* — usually the layup.

This decision happens in less than a second. Hesitating is the same as being wrong.

## The trap most low men fall into

They wait too long. They want to see the ball get all the way to the paint before they move. By then, the offense has won — even if you stop the layup, the kick-out three is wide open because nobody rotated behind you.

**Move on the read, not the result.**

## The X-out

When the low man rotates over, the **next** weak-side defender has to rotate to the open shooter. This is called an X-out because the two defenders cross paths. If anyone in the chain is late or quiet, the offense scores easy.

Calls you need:
- **"I got ball!"** from the low man
- **"I got your man!"** from the next rotator
- **"Skip!"** if the ball is being kicked across the floor

## What you're training

The low-man scenarios put you on the weak side as the play breaks down. You have a heartbeat to choose: take the ball, tag the roller, or stay home. The wrong call costs the team. The right call makes you the most valuable defender on the floor.

## Coach's cue

> *"Low man saves the play. Talk loud, move early, trust the next."*

Great low men are why great defenses give up jump shots instead of layups.
